blocks.content: The Hartwell Fairgrounds were at the edge of town where the land went flat and wide, and the carnival rose from the landscape the way carnivals do — improbable and slightly defiant, insisting on itself against the ordinary background of fields and sky.

You heard it before you saw it fully. The particular texture of carnival sound — music overlapping music, the mechanical call of ride operators, something indistinct and joyful that didn't resolve into any specific sound but became the atmosphere of the whole.
Then the lights, even in daylight: the strings of them along the midway, the colored bulbs of the rides, the Ferris wheel visible from the parking lot, turning slowly against the blue sky.

The group arrived in two cars and convened in the parking lot with the specific energy that fairgrounds produce in adults who have forgotten, briefly, that they were allowed to enjoy things without explanation.

Nathan had found his carnival energy before they even reached the gate. Maya was wearing sunglasses and claiming neutrality while visibly calculating which way the funnel cake smell was coming from. Lena was quiet in her particular way — attentive, clocking things.
Ethan found Cassandra in the parking lot crowd and fell into step beside her without ceremony. He didn't take her hand or say anything. He just walked beside her.
She looked up at the Ferris wheel.
He watched her look at it.
